Robust Infrastructure Scheduling
SYSEN 5211 · Anurag Yadav · Spring 2026
Resume
Flagship · Optimisation · MIP · Monte Carlo

Schedule construction and traffic as one system.

Urban corridor work runs inside live traffic. This project couples a 24-task construction schedule with four daily traffic blocks, optimises cost and disruption jointly, and stress-tests the plan with 10,000 Monte Carlo runs per schedule variant.

−27.3% Traffic disruption
−$35K Mean cost vs baseline
10,000 MC iterations / variant
0 Extra resources
01 · Problem

Why separate Gantt charts fail

Three properties make joint planning hard. Each is a card you can read on its own.

Feedback loops

Lane closures add congestion, which slows the work causing the closure. Traffic and construction co-evolve during the day.

Coupled dynamics

Cascade amplification

A one-day slip upstream does not stay one day downstream. Delays propagate superlinearly through the dependency network.

Highest sensitivity param

Cost–time coupling

Peak-window work raises labour cost, idle equipment cost, and public disruption at the same time. You cannot split those effects cleanly.

Block assignment matters
02 · Model

What was built

MIP schedule, robust scenario layer, and Monte Carlo validation: three layers side by side.

MIP core

Mixed integer program with 121 decision variables: 24 task start days, 96 binary block assignments, one makespan.

  • 24 interdependent tasks, 40-day corridor horizon
  • Four daily blocks (B1–B4) with delay multipliers per task
  • Objective: labour cost + makespan + traffic disruption (weighted)
  • Solved in PuLP/CBC, ≤8% gap, under 30 s on a laptop

Robust extension

Robust objective blends expected cost across four disruption scenarios with worst-case weight λ = 0.35.

  • S1 nominal (50%) through S4 worst-case (10%)
  • λ-sweep showed flat P90 cost: schedule is robust by structure, not conservative weighting
  • Peak-avoidance strategy identical at λ = 0 and λ = 1

Monte Carlo

10,000 iterations per schedule (30,000 total) with eight calibrated uncertainty sources.

  • Task duration, traffic DM, deliveries, equipment, weather, labour
  • Optimised schedule dominates baseline at every percentile (P90, P95, P99)
  • Cascade multiplier Triangular(1.0, 1.2, 1.6) drives tail risk

B1 · 06–07

Off-peak early · lowest DM

B2 · 07–09

AM peak · DM up to ~1.72

B3 · 09–16

Midday off-peak

B4 · 16–18

PM peak

05 · FAQ

Common questions

Short answers shown directly.

What does block assignment mean?

Each task picks one of four daily blocks. The optimiser chooses the block, not the planner. Delay multipliers encode how congestion changes effective duration (e.g. T07 in B2 at DM 1.72 vs B1 at 1.18).

Why is cascade amplification important?

Upstream overruns trigger a multiplier on successors (Triangular 1.0–1.6). It was the highest-sensitivity parameter in the study. Buffering before T13 cut activation rate sharply.

Did robust λ actually change the schedule?

No meaningful change. P90 cost and duration were flat from λ = 0 to 1. Peak avoidance at the deterministic optimum already carried the robustness.

Where are Pareto and sensitivity plots?

Tradeoff curves, tornado charts, and block Gantt views are in the full technical section. Open it from the link at the end of this overview.

Technical report

View full technical section

Equations, constraint tables, Monte Carlo distributions, Pareto and sensitivity figures, quantified decisions, limitations, and conclusions from the course submission.

07 · Technical depth

Formulation, uncertainty, figures, and conclusions

The sections below preserve the full case study: equations, constraint tables, Monte Carlo distributions, Pareto and sensitivity analysis, quantified decisions, limitations, and embedded figures from the course submission.

01

Problem & Systems Framing

Urban infrastructure construction does not occur in isolation. Every lane closure, material delivery, and equipment movement takes place inside a live, congested traffic system simultaneously moving tens of thousands of vehicles. These two systems are strongly coupled: decisions in one propagate into the other through mechanisms that are nonlinear, time-dependent, and systematically underestimated by conventional planning tools.

In current practice this coupling is managed reactively at best, and ignored at worst. Project managers build Gantt schedules based on task logic, crew availability, and unit costs. Traffic engineers design diversion plans based on volume data. These are rarely produced jointly and almost never optimised together.

Three system properties make this resistant to conventional scheduling: (1) Feedback loops: traffic congestion caused by a lane closure lengthens the task causing it, which worsens congestion. (2) Emergent cascade behaviour: a one-day upstream delay does not produce a one-day project delay; it amplifies superlinearly through the dependency network. (3) Cost-time coupling: peak-window tasks simultaneously drive up labour cost, idle equipment cost, and societal traffic cost.

Six study objectives pursued: data-driven system representation from real-world documents → MIP co-optimising scheduling, block assignment, and resources → 10,000-iteration Monte Carlo validation → robust optimisation extension (4 scenarios, λ=0.35) → sensitivity and tradeoff analysis → four specific actionable decisions.

02

Mathematical Formulation

A deterministic MIP is formulated first as the basis for stochastic evaluation and robust extension. The standard progression in optimisation under uncertainty.

Decision variables (Eq. 1)
st ∈ ℤ⁺, [0, HMAX] — start day of task t
btk ∈ {0,1} — 1 if task t assigned to block k
mk ∈ ℤ⁺, [0, HMAX] — project makespan (days)
Objective function (Eq. 7)
Minimise: Z = α·Ctotal + β·mk·Ω + γ·Ctraf
where α=1.00 (cost), β=0.30 (duration), γ=0.50 (traffic), Ω=8,000
Robust formulation (Eq. 8)
Zrob = (1−λ)·ES[Z] + λ·ZS4
λ=0.35 (risk-aversion) — λ=0: expected cost only; λ=1: worst-case only (minimax)

Model scale: 121 decision variables, 9 constraint classes (~300 individual constraints), solved with PuLP/CBC in under 30 seconds at ≤8% MIP gap.

ConstraintEquationRole
C1Σk btk = 1Exactly one block per task
C2sj ≥ si + pdiFinish-to-start precedence (25 edges)
C3sj ≥ siStart-to-start precedence (8 edges)
C4mk ≥ st + pdtMakespan covers all task completions
C5st + pdt ≤ 55Hard horizon limit (HMAX=55)
C6Σ wt·pdt·btk ≤ capr(w)·5·2Resource pool (weekly, per resource type)
C7–C8Non-negativity + integralityDomain constraints
C9mk ≤ mcapPareto sweep: active when tracing frontier
S1 · Nominal
No disruption
Prob: 50%
Duration ×: 1.00
Traffic ×: 1.00
Cost ×: 1.00
S2 · Moderate
Peak + 1 weather event
Prob: 25%
Duration ×: 1.15
Traffic ×: 1.35
Cost ×: 1.18
S3 · High
Equipment + material + peak
Prob: 15%
Duration ×: 1.30
Traffic ×: 1.60
Cost ×: 1.35
S4 · Worst-case
All risks + cascade active
Prob: 10%
Duration ×: 1.50
Traffic ×: 1.80
Cost ×: 1.55
03

Uncertainty Modelling

Eight independent uncertainty sources are modelled with calibrated probability distributions. Each drawn from observed project data rather than assumed generic distributions.

SourceDistributionMeanNotes
Task duration multiplierTriangular(1.00, 1.12, 1.45)1.14Calibrated from 24 actual vs planned durations
Traffic DM (peak block)Uniform(1.30, 1.80)1.53Wide range reflects day-to-day peak variation
Traffic DM (off-peak)Uniform(1.00, 1.20)1.08Tight range (midday traffic predictable)
Traffic volume noiseNormal(1.0, 0.08)1.00Multiplicative noise on base hourly volume
Material delivery delayBernoulli(0.25) × Geometric(0.75)1.4 days if triggered25% failure probability; 5 observed events
Equipment breakdownBernoulli(0.05/machine-day)—Cost: Normal(13000, 2500); Delay: {1,2} days
Weather shutdownBernoulli(0.08/week)—Duration: Triangular(0.5, 1.5, 3.0) days if triggered
Labour absenteeismBeta(1.5, 10.0)9%Right-skewed (most days normal, occasional spikes)
Cascade amplification model (Eq. 18): When any task overruns its planned duration, all successor tasks are affected: actualj = max(actualj, pdj·casc + (actuali − pdi)·0.40), where casc ~ Triangular(1.0, 1.2, 1.6). This produces the empirically-observed superlinear relationship between upstream delays and total project delay. Cascade multiplier is the highest-sensitivity parameter in the model.
04

Results: Three-Way Comparison

Mean cost reduction−$35K−2.8% vs baseline
P90 cost reduction−$50K−3.3% vs baseline
Traffic disruption−27.3%Index 592 → 430

The Gantt comparison shows the structural difference: the MIP moves all high-impact tasks from peak blocks (B2, red outline) to the early off-peak window (B1). Task order remains identical; only the time-of-day window changes. This demonstrates that scheduling granularity below the day level is the critical modelling decision, not resource quantity or task sequencing.

Full stochastic comparison across n=10,000 iterations per variant. Cost distributions shift 2.8% leftward from baseline to optimised, with standard deviation decreasing by $4,964. The CDFs (panels D, E) show the optimised and robust solutions dominate the baseline at every percentile. Variance decomposition (panel F) confirms std dev reduction across all three metrics.

05

Tail Risk, Pareto Frontiers & λ-Sweep

P50-to-P99 tail risk comparison (panels A, B) and CVaR curve (panel C). CVaR measures E[cost | cost ≥ threshold]. The expected cost in the tail above each confidence level. The shaded region between baseline and optimised represents tail-risk savings that grow at higher confidence levels. P99 cost reduction: $1,825K → $1,800K.

Three tradeoff dimensions. Panel A (cost-duration Pareto): compressing makespan below 49 days costs an extra $58,729 (+7.3%); the 49–55-day range is cost-equivalent. Optimised solution sits at the efficient knee. Panel B (cost-traffic): convex tradeoff, reducing traffic disruption from 311 to 258 units costs an additional $119,562 (+14.8%). First 10 tasks moved off-peak provide most benefit; last 5 add diminishing returns. Panel C (risk-cost): E[cost] and P90[cost] both improve together, optimised dominates baseline in both dimensions simultaneously.

Key λ-sweep finding: P90 cost, P90 duration, and deterministic cost are essentially flat across λ=0 to λ=1. The schedule is robust by construction, peak-avoidance structurally reduces variance. λ=0.35 is a presentation convention for the robust formulation, not a critical calibration parameter. The deterministic model already discovers the same strategy as the robust formulation.
06

Four Actionable Decisions

All four decisions are expressible without additional technology or resources. Source: actionable_decisions.csv. The optimised schedule is recommended over the robust variant. It delivers equivalent expected performance at no additional complexity.

Decision 1
Reschedule 12 high-impact tasks: peak → B1 (06:00–07:00)
Every task with road_impact ≥ 2 moves from B2 (AM peak) to B1 (early off-peak). Top five moves: T13 drainage_installation ($20,160, 3.36 days), T07 pavement_milling_A ($25,920, 2.16 days), T08 pavement_milling_B ($25,920, 2.16 days), T03 utility_relocation_gas ($16,200, 2.25 days), T20 asphalt_paving_B ($22,230, 1.71 days).
Cumulative: 19.0 aggregate days saved · $166,451 total cost saving across 12 tasks
Decision 2
Add 2-day float before T13 (critical cascade gate)
T13 (drainage_installation_seg_A) sits at the junction of the T07→T10→T13 upstream path and the T13→T16→T19 downstream path, and both highest-cost, highest-impact streams. A 1-day equipment breakdown at T07 (prob 5%/machine-day) propagates to T13 with cascade ×1.2–1.6, then continues to all paving tasks. Pre-scheduling a 2-day buffer absorbs single-event breakdowns without triggering cascade propagation.
Cascade activation probability: 42% → 18%
Decision 3
Stagger specialist crew assignments in weeks 3–5
The specialist crew pool (max 12 units, weeks 3–5) is the binding resource constraint in 68% of Monte Carlo baseline runs. Tasks T10/T11/T12 (subbase preparation) and T13/T14/T15 (drainage installation) overlap in resource demand. Staggering T11 start by +1 day and T14 start by +1 day reduces peak demand from 14 to 10 units within the pool limit, without extending the critical path.
Crew binding constraint: 68% of MC runs → eliminated
Decision 4
Monitor T24, lone remaining peak-block assignment
T24 (final_inspection_handover) is the only task the MIP assigns to a peak block (B2) in the optimised solution. Its road_impact is 0. It does not cause traffic disruption, so the assignment is cost-acceptable. However, as the final dependency-chain node, any overrun directly extends project delivery. Flag T24 start date in the weekly progress tracker for the final two project weeks.
Only peak-block task remaining · road_impact = 0 · final delivery gate
07

Discussion

Why the optimizer worked: DM as a decision variable
The baseline treats delay multiplier as fixed background noise. The optimizer treats it as a degree of freedom. T07 in AM peak (B2) carries DM=1.72. Workers on-site, progressing at 58% uncongested rate. The same task at 06:00–07:00 (B1) carries DM=1.18. The optimizer consistently preferred B1 across all γ values above 0.25, a sharp threshold, not a gradual response.
Also less variable, not only cheaper
By reducing DM variance (peak DMs range 1.30–1.80 vs off-peak 1.00–1.20), the optimised schedule is exposed to narrower uncertainty. This directly reduces P90 cost from $1.507M to $1.457M, a $50,360 tail-risk improvement, even though mean durations are nearly identical. The improvement in expected value and variance reduction are both achieved simultaneously.
Pareto knee interpretation
The cost-duration frontier confirms the optimised makespan of 51 days is appropriate: compression below 49 days is expensive ($9,800/day), compression above 49 days is costless. The optimal solution sits precisely at the efficient knee, further compression is expensive, further relaxation is free. No additional time is needed to realise the full cost benefit.
Trade-off guidelines for decision-makers
γ above 0.25 eliminates peak-block usage entirely; no need to tune above 0.50, confirmed in sensitivity analysis. The robust premium is negligible: recommend always using the robust formulation since the cost is zero. Schedule compression below 49 days: ~$9,800/day. Cost-traffic tradeoff is convex: first 10 tasks moved off-peak provide most benefit.
Four-block DM approximation
Continuous traffic modelling would improve DM precision but requires a non-linear programming formulation. The four-block abstraction captures the dominant effect but misses intra-block variation.
Cascade calibration uncertainty
Cascade amplification Triangular(1.0–1.6) is the highest-sensitivity parameter but calibrated from only 22 risk events. Larger empirical datasets would reduce this uncertainty significantly.
Static information assumption
The model optimises start decisions once with fixed information. Rolling-horizon re-optimisation would further reduce variance but requires a different architecture. Suggested as direct extension.
Societal cost monetisation
σ=$3,200/hr is uncertain. Qualitative conclusions are stable to ±50% variation in this parameter. The peak-avoidance strategy is robust to this uncertainty.
08

Conclusions

Central finding: integrating traffic-aware block scheduling into construction planning, treating the delay multiplier as a decision variable, reduces P90 project cost by 3.3% ($50K), mean cost by 2.8% ($35K), and traffic disruption index by 27.3%, without additional resources, acceleration, or technology.

The strategy is structurally robust: sensitivity analysis confirms peak-avoidance dominates across all tested λ and γ values. In this system, when work happens matters more than how it is optimised.

This study integrates deterministic optimisation, stochastic evaluation, and robust optimisation into a unified decision framework: the deterministic MIP provides the optimal nominal schedule; Monte Carlo simulation reveals distributional risk; the robust formulation provides formal risk-adjusted protection against adverse scenarios.

Future work: Digital twin integration with real-time traffic sensors and IoT site data would enable rolling-horizon re-optimisation. The data pipeline architecture (CSV → Python → MIP) is already compatible. Replacing the four-block DM with a Bureau of Public Roads flow function would improve precision. Multi-project corridor optimisation would extend the framework to a city-level infrastructure programme.
